title: Banks Don't Lend Money — They Create It: The Myth That Runs the World Economy

source.original: Where Does Money Come From? — Josh Ryan-Collins, Tony Greenham, Richard Werner, Andrew Jackson

body_html:
The most consequential lie taught in economics classrooms worldwide is also one of the simplest: that banks are intermediaries. That they collect deposits from savers and lend that pool of money to borrowers. It sounds intuitive. It is wrong. And the consequences of this widespread misunderstanding reach into housing markets, monetary policy, financial crises, and the political economy of who gets credit and who doesn't.

The actual mechanics of bank lending are different enough from the textbook version to be startling once you see them clearly. When a commercial bank makes a loan, it does not transfer money from one account to another. It creates two entries simultaneously: a new asset (the loan on its books) and a new liability (a deposit in the borrower's account). The money did not exist before. No saver was made poorer. No reserve was drawn down to fund it. The bank manufactured new purchasing power through a double-entry accounting operation. This is not a fringe heterodox claim — it is now formally acknowledged by the Bank of England itself.

The textbook alternative — the money multiplier model — says that central banks create base money (reserves), commercial banks lend multiples of that base, and the money supply expands in a predictable ratio determined by the reserve requirement. This model has a satisfying mechanical elegance. It implies that the central bank controls the money supply by controlling reserves. It implies that banks are constrained by prior savings. All of it is backwards from how the system actually works.

In reality, banks extend credit based on loan demand, creditworthiness assessment, and profit expectations. They do not check their reserve positions before making loans — they make the loans and then acquire the reserves they need afterward in the interbank market or from the central bank directly. The Bank of England, the Federal Reserve, and every other major central bank act as accommodators of reserve demand, not controllers of the money supply. The causality runs opposite to what every introductory macroeconomics course teaches.

This matters because it reframes what banks fundamentally are. They are not financial intermediaries — they are money creators with a government license. The privilege of money creation was historically a sovereign power. Modern states have largely delegated this power to private institutions operating for profit, subject to regulation that — as the 2008 crisis demonstrated — was insufficient to prevent catastrophic misallocation.

The implications for monetary policy are severe. If central banks don't control the money supply, then interest rate decisions work through a different and much more indirect transmission mechanism than the standard models imply. Quantitative Easing — the process of flooding the banking system with reserves — was based on the hope that more reserves would translate into more lending. It didn't, because reserves aren't the binding constraint on lending. Banks don't lend because they have reserves; they lend when they identify profitable lending opportunities with acceptable credit risk. QE inflated asset prices, widened wealth inequality, and did comparatively little for the real economy. The model was wrong.

The parallel with crypto is worth noting. Bitcoin was designed explicitly with a fixed supply and transparent issuance rules — a reaction to the opacity and elasticity of fiat money creation. The premise underlying Satoshi's white paper was that money creation by private banks backed by state guarantees was a system prone to abuse and opacity. Understanding how banks actually create money makes that reaction more legible, even if you think decentralized alternatives have their own failure modes. The debate about who should create money, and under what rules, is not new — Bitcoin just restarted it in public.
